resources.string: • Parallel Lines: Two straight lines in space that lie in one plane and have no common points. • Skew Lines: Two straight lines in space that do not lie in one plane. • Angle Between Skew Lines: The angle between intersecting lines that are respectively parallel to the given skew lines. • Perpendicular Line to a Plane: A straight line cutting a plane that is perpendicular to any straight line lying in that plane. • Distance Between Parallel Planes: The length of the segment cut off by the planes on any common perpendicular.
Do skew lines lie in the same plane?

• Perpendicularity Test (Theorem 1): If a straight line cutting a plane is perpendicular to two intersecting lines in that plane, it is perpendicular to the entire plane. • Parallelism of Planes (Theorem 2): If two intersecting lines of one plane are respectively parallel to two intersecting lines of another plane, the planes are parallel. • Plane Separation: Parallel planes are equidistant; any perpendicular segment between them is congruent (e.g., |A1A2|=|B1B2|). • Angle of Line/Plane: The angle between a line and its projection onto the plane.
How many intersecting lines in a plane must a line be perpendicular to in order to be perpendicular to the entire plane?

• Problem 1 (Angle Between Skew Lines): Find the angle between diagonals of adjacent faces of a cube. By drawing a line parallel to one diagonal to intersect the other, an equilateral triangle is formed, proving the angle is 60°. \n • Problem 2 (Mutual Perpendiculars): Construction of three mutually perpendicular lines a, b, and c. By establishing line OA perpendicular to plane α (containing a, b) and line OD perpendicular to OB, the three axes are proven perpendicular via Theorem 1.
What is the angle (in degrees) between the diagonals of two adjacent faces of a cube?

• Polyhedron: A solid bounded by plane polygons called faces; intersections of faces are edges, and intersections of edges are vertices. • Right Prism: A polyhedron whose bases are parallel congruent polygons and whose lateral edges are perpendicular to the bases. • Rectangular Parallelepiped: A right prism whose base is a rectangle. • Cube: A rectangular parallelepiped where all twelve edges are equal. • Pyramid: A polyhedron consisting of a polygon (base) and a vertex not in the base plane, joined by triangular lateral faces. • Regular Pyramid: A pyramid with a regular polygon base whose vertex projects onto the centre of the base.
How many edges does a cube have?

• Prism Area: Lateral area S_lat=P×h (perimeter × altitude); Total area S=S_lat+2×S_base. • Parallelepiped Diagonal: The square of any diagonal equals the sum of squares of its three dimensions (d²=a²+b²+c²). • Volume: For a rectangular parallelepiped V=a×b×c; for any right prism V=S_base×h. • Pyramid Area/Volume: Lateral area of a regular pyramid S_lat=(P×h_lat)/2 (where h_lat is slant height); Volume of any pyramid V=(S_base×h)/3.
A rectangular parallelepiped has dimensions 3, 4, and 5. What is the square of its diagonal (d²)?

• Right prism with square base of side 4 and height 6: lateral area = 4 × 4 × 6 = 96; base area = 16; total area = 96 + 2 × 16 = 128. \n • Volume of the same prism: V = 4² × 6 = 96. \n • Regular square pyramid with base side 6, slant height 5: lateral area = (4 × 6 × 5) / 2 = 60; base area = 36; total area = 96. Volume with height 4: V = (36 × 4) / 3 = 48.
What is the volume of a pyramid with base area 36 and height 4?

• Cylinder: A solid generated by line segments (elements) perpendicular to two parallel planes and bounded by congruent circles. \n • Cone: A solid generated by joining all points of a circle to a vertex S; the line from the vertex to the base center is the axis. \n • Sphere: All points in space at a distance not exceeding R from a center O; its boundary is the sphere.
Is the axis of a cone the line segment from the vertex to the centre of the base?

• Cylinder: Lateral area S_lat=2πRh; Total area S=2πRh+2πR²; Volume V=πR²h. 
 • Cone: Lateral area S_lat=πRl (where l=slant height=√(R²+h²)); Total area S=πRl+πR²; Volume V=(πR²h)/3. 
 • Sphere: Surface area S=4πR²; Volume V=(4/3)πR³. 
 • A plane intersects a sphere in a circle; the largest such circle (through centre O) is a great circle.
What is the volume of a cone with base radius R = 3 and height h = 4 (leave answer in terms of π)?

• Cylinder with R = 3 and h = 5: lateral area = 2π × 3 × 5 = 30π; total area = 30π + 2π × 9 = 48π; volume = π × 9 × 5 = 45π. \n • Cone with R = 3, h = 4: slant height l = √(9 + 16) = 5; lateral area = π × 3 × 5 = 15π; total area = 15π + 9π = 24π; volume = (π × 9 × 4) / 3 = 12π. \n • Sphere with R = 6: surface area = 4π × 36 = 144π; volume = (4/3) × π × 216 = 288π.
What is the surface area of a sphere with radius R = 6 (leave answer in terms of π)?

• Coordinate System in Space: Three mutually perpendicular axes (x, y, z) meeting at the origin O; every point is described by an ordered triple (x, y, z). \n • Vector in Space: A directed segment defined by magnitude and direction; denoted AB or a⃗ with components (x, y, z). \n • Distance Between Two Points: |A1A2| = √((x2−x1)²+(y2−y1)²+(z2−z1)²).

• Vector addition: a⃗ + b⃗ = (a1+b1, a2+b2, a3+b3). 
 • Scalar multiplication: k×a⃗ = (k×a1, k×a2, k×a3). 
 • Dot product: a⃗·b⃗ = a1b1+a2b2+a3b3; if a⃗·b⃗ = 0, the vectors are perpendicular. 
 • Magnitude: |a⃗| = √(a1²+a2²+a3²). 
 • Midpoint of segment A(x1,y1,z1) and B(x2,y2,z2): M = ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2, (z1+z2)/2).
